title: The Dot-Com Bubble
summary: A rapid rise in U.S. equity valuations fueled by investments in Internet-based companies. Speculation and an abundance of venture capital funding for startups led to a massive bubble that eventually burst, wiping out trillions in market value.
impact: Led to the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 to protect investors from the possibility of fraudulent accounting activities by corporations. Shifted investor focus from 'eyeballs' back to traditional metrics like cash flow and profitability.

title: The Global Financial Crisis
summary: Triggered by a large decline in US home prices after the collapse of a housing bubble, leading to mortgage delinquencies, foreclosures, and the devaluation of housing-related securities.
impact: Resulted in the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act. Introduced massive quantitative easing (QE) programs by central banks globally and fundamentally changed banking capital requirements (Basel III).

title: Black Monday
summary: On October 19, 1987, stock markets around the world crashed, shedding a huge value in a very short time. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) fell exactly 508 points to 1,738.74 (22.61%).
impact: Led to the introduction of 'circuit breakers' (trading curbs) to prevent similar massive panic sell-offs. Highlighted the dangers of programmatic trading without human oversight.

title: Asian Financial Crisis
summary: A period of financial crisis that gripped much of East Asia and Southeast Asia beginning in July 1997 and raised fears of a worldwide economic meltdown due to financial contagion.
impact: Led to massive IMF bailouts with strict structural adjustment conditions. Many Asian nations built up massive foreign exchange reserves to prevent future currency attacks.

title: The Enron Scandal
summary: Enron Corporation, an American energy, commodities, and services company, was revealed to have engaged in massive accounting fraud, hiding billions of dollars in debt from failed deals and projects.
impact: Led to the bankruptcy of Enron and the dissolution of Arthur Andersen. Was a primary catalyst for the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, drastically increasing the penalties for destroying, altering, or fabricating records in federal investigations.

title: Tulip Mania
summary: A period during the Dutch Golden Age when contract prices for some bulbs of the recently introduced and fashionable tulip reached extraordinarily high levels, and then dramatically collapsed.
impact: Often cited as the first recorded speculative bubble. While its economic impact was limited, it remains a powerful psychological case study on the madness of crowds and speculative excess.
